Workers working less does not necessarily reduce productivity; in fact, it can increase productivity per worker due to mechanization and automation. In GR, what is viewed as a force is the deviation from a geodesic (straight line in curved spacetime) due to non-gravitational interactions, such as electromagnetic or contact forces. The gravitational effect itself is not a force but a manifestation of spacetime curvature. However, when an object is prevented from following a geodesic (e.g., by a surface pushing up on it), the resulting acceleration relative to free-fall is interpreted as a force. Thus, the "gravitational force" in GR is not a fundamental force but an apparent force arising from the object's resistance to following the natural curved path of spacetime. The perceived force is the result of the object's momentum changing due to external constraints, not gravity itself.
